speaker: interviewer
text: David Steinweis. And you were talking about the death march as it was approaching Buchenwald.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we came in to Buchenwald. All-- it was missing already a lot of people. And we went out 10,000. When we came there, 2,000 was a lot. And then, when we came in there, I was lucky again. I got-- they took me into building 49. And over there was people-- not Jewish, from Hollandale. Some of them maybe were Jewish, the other one. And they treat me there good. And from there, I was then maybe a week, two. And from there, they went me to Berga/Elster.

speaker: interviewee
text: Berga/Elster-- they called this a [NON-ENGLISH]. We were working in the hills to make tunnels. And over there, they want to make factory from ammunition. And over there, when a group came, they could only live four weeks. And I was lucky again. A German, the foreman, he was hitting me. And I said to him, why you hit me? He said, because you don't want to work. I said, I am not used to this work. I said, what you did before? I told him I am a tailor. He said, a tailor? Good. I give you tailoring work. And this helped me a lot. He took me in the [NON-ENGLISH]. He brought me everything what I need. I made him [NON-ENGLISH], hats.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I don't know what he did with them. Maybe he sold it. And then I took in, in the camp. And I gave to other cap makers, what I made, the-- make this. And I delivered to him maybe 20 hat-- hat a week, 25 hats a week. And in this I helped me to survive. I got more food, and more food for my comrades, too. From Berga/Elster we went-- again, the American came from the other side. And we have, again to-- to-- they pulled us out from there, the Nazis-- again, the death march. Then we were on the way to Theresienstadt. It was worse than before to Buchenwald. They keep shooting on the people, because the people were tired. They could not walk. It was terrible. And we came-- OK, I will leave this. And we came, on the way, to Thersienstadt. It was not so easy-- or a group of people came to Theresienstadt.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not to Theresienstadt. Yeah, to Theresienstadt, yeah. It's good, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people started that death march? How many ended it?

speaker: interviewee
text: 10,020. I was from a group from 10,000. It was 60 people. They checked us in Karlsbad. I mentioned Karlsbad before? Yeah. Over there, we were 120 people. 60 people was not Jewish. They were politicians, communists, other politicians against the Nazis. And 60 people were Jewish. And I-- I am-- from the 60 Jewish people, what I survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you got Theresienstadt. And how long were you there? And what happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Over there, when we came to Theresienstadt, we were living-- and already, I was living on the second floor. Maybe it was there 200 people. And over there was people from Hungarian-- the Jewish girls. I mean, I didn't see the children. And it was not so good there. Only we got at least food. We got in the morning soup, then-- and then it came-- it was-- on May 8th came in the Russian, 8 o'clock in the morning. And they liberated us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like? What happened? What was that day like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, when they liberated us, then our people-- some of them, they want to go out and to teach a lesson that-- Germans. Then the-- the-- the Russian officers did not advise that to do it. One they-- they went on the table and he got a speech. He said, don't go out. You survived. You are alive. OK, you could be killed from the Russians, too. Because we will  not allow that civilians got to take advantage of that, to kill other people. We are here. We are-- this is the law, our law, to watch that you will be safe. On and-- I will not allowed it, he said, to go out even one person to do something bad to the Germans. We did-- I mean, nobody went out. Only I went out right away with the Russian tank. I didn't want to be there anymore, because it was the sickness-- typhus, other sickness. And I saw people falling and they're dying. And I was scared from that. Then, right away, I talked with a Russian tanker. And I told him, take me on the tank and take me out of here. He said, I will take you to the wall. And you go down, and you go on that wall, and you can jump over. Only I will not take you out, because it's not allowed. And then he took me to the wall. And I jumped out. It was not allowed for [INAUDIBLE] to go out, because the sickness was so bad there, because they didn't let out not even one person from there.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I jumped over, it was the Czechoslovakian policeman after the war. And he said to me, why are you doing this? I said, I'm going only to the farmer, to something for food. I will be right back. He said, well, how long it will take? And I said, maybe an hour. And I never came back.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to Poland this time. And I-- it-- it took me maybe 10 days, or 12 days, to come to Poland, back to Sosnowitz [NON-ENGLISH]. I came to [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you see when you saw-- when you came back-- when you came back to your home town, what did you find?

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife. It was a few what they came back to look for families-- a few girls and a few men. That's what-- maybe all of us together it was maybe 10 or 12. And I found a brother who was living in Myslowice there, not far. Because he was liberated in January from the Russian. They-- my brother, he died for a year and a  half ago.

speaker: interviewee
text: I am from a family from seven people. And I am the only one what I am alive. And two died in this country-- two-- one for-- how long is Rachmil dead? 20 years? And-- and the other one a year-- maybe a year and a half. And this is from the old family. I am only alone from the old family. So you met your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or you met your girlfriend and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. I called her. And I was helping to organize to go back to Germany. In Germany, we got married, in Deggendorf. I want to make this fast, I know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now the DP camp-- what was life like for you in the DP camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the DP camp-- it was a free-- OK, we rested there. We didn't do nothing. OK, I went there, to the farmers, the-- I wanted to-- to get some better food. I went to them, just to bring for myself. And it was not bad in Deggendorf. The UNRRA took care on us. And from there, we registered to go to United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you came to the US--

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- we came, yeah. On November 29, we came to the US. And this and that day, what UN recognized to divide that land there-- Israel and then Jordan, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: 1 life in the US? Were the people friendly, open to you? Was it-- was-- were they negative to you? What kind of reaction did you get?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was not so easy for us. See, because we-- we didn't got money. And I went to federation. And they said, ask the-- the family to go where, my wife's family. And they said, they have to take care. Then I got tired from running. And I went to work. And I worked every day, six days a week, until, little by little, we got something. Then they helped us only to get a little apartment. And later on, when I got more money, we changed it for better. And then we got that little boy then we have to raise. And we were, from the beginning, happy. I was working. And my wife-- she got to stay home to take care on the-- it was a baby. The baby was not even a year old. And I would not say I was not happy. When I-- we came to this country, I was right away happy. I knew it's a free country. And I knew I can work. And when you're working, OK, you can't become a millionaire, only you can make a living here.

speaker: interviewer
text: The other Jews in America treated you nicely? Or didn't treat you nice-- other Jews here, other non-Jews here, they treat you nice?

speaker: interviewee
text: Neighbors-- I got good neighbors that they helped me with watching the kid when my wife got to go someplace. I will-- I will not complain that some-- somebody treat me bad, because I want to be always independent, not to get from something for nothing, when I am free and I can work. It was not so easy to make a living. Because in this time, from the beginning, they paid $1 an hour. 40 hours is $40. And rent we got cheap. It was $27 rent. Only still, in money, it was not so good. Only later on, I was looking for other job, I made $70, $80 then. Then I was smarter. I went from the men's shops to the lady's work, to the Seventh Avenue New York, to make more money. And then later on, I went in dry cleaning business. In the dry cleaning business, OK, you can't get very rich, you know, but you made a good living.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you think living through the Holocaust changed you? Did it make you less trusting, more trusting? Did it-- how'd it change as you as a-- as a person?

speaker: interviewee
text: With the-- what do you mean, with the religion? Oh--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, first, just in general. I mean, are you a more frightened person? More outgoing? Less outgoing? Or did it change you at all-- made you less trusting? More trusting? Did it change you at all, having been  through all of that?

speaker: interviewee
text: You can't-- you know, I was talking this week with my wife about it's very hard to find the-- a very near friend-- very hard. I have friends. Only you can't tell them everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because why? What can't you tell them? What-- what do you mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- maybe they are not too honest to keep a secret. Or you tell somebody on your life. And they tell somebody else. And then everybody will know. And they-- and later on, then it's much better I-- I live with my friends not in secret, only open. And I'm going for justice. Even here, in this building, I was two years on the board. And now I'm running again, to do something, because I have nothing to do. And I always go for justice. I-- I don't want to see somebody cheat somebody else, or to tell-- somebody tells lies, something like that. I will-- I want the truth and justice.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about religion? Did going through the Holocaust make you a more devout Jew? A less devout Jew? Stronger belief in God? Less belief in God?

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, in this war, it was a war, but never it was a war like that, especially for Jewish people. I never saw a miracle. They took people, and they killed, without [NON-ENGLISH], or how you say. And-- and they did, the Nazis, what they want. And never there have been something they got punished from God, something like that. And they said to us, you have a God. And they got on the belt here, written, that they believe in God. We-- our God is good. Your God is no good. And they-- you're thinking about religion. You see, you can't be Orthodox after this. Maybe we have a God, maybe. I would not say we don't have. It was a miracle that I survived, or others like me. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you had to-- here's a moment to talk to your grandchildren and great grandchildren, or whoever will be watching this tape. What kind of message would you want to tell people about yourself, about the war, about-- [SIGHS] what's the point you would want to-- to get across?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I always advised that my children have not to go to war, to fight. Even my son-- he was in the reserve. And to be honest with people, and-- and I told you before, I believe in justice and I believe in the truth, and to live a quiet, good life in peace. And what else I can say? OK, we-- we watching the television. We're reading newspapers. We hope it's going to be peace in Israel and peace in the whole world. And war is no good.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, are you optimistic? Do you think a Holocaust will never happen again? Or do you think something like this may happen again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we can work on that [INAUDIBLE] not to happen. Because in this country, we have the Ku Klux Klan. And we have other anti-Semites. And we have other people-- troublemakers. We have to work not to let them to go too high and to take over the power. Because we saw in Europe how the countries were with other powers what they believed, like in Poland. It was a democratic party-- party only for them, not for the Jews. Because the Jew was a second-class citizen. In other countries, OK, it's good to be a democrat. Only it's good to be a good democrat, not just a democrat-- how would they call this-- like in Russia is today. Here, in this country, is all right. People working hard. And the politicians that-- we've got to stick to the Democratic Party. And I remember my father used to tell me, you go-- you-- when you will have a chance, go to America. Over there is the democratic everything. And this is a good party. He understood that. And my uncle told me the same thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you optimistic? Do you think things will be OK for your-- It depends on all people in the United States. When they go-- when they give them the power to do something bad to other people-- the Nazis or the other one-- we have here a lot of them. And the propaganda, what they are doing this, and we hear from-- from other countries against Jewish people, or against other people. And I will talk about the colored people. It will be when the Jewish people will be united with the colored people. Because they were once slaves, too. And they were-- and they are people like we are, only they are black, and we are white. When we will be united with them, it will be much better for us. Because you can talk to the Ku Klux Klan like that. You-- they coming on television or they talk about-- about the Jewish people or colored people. And we let them. It's a free country-- freedom of speech. Only it's too much freedom here. OK, I think that's a good stop right there, OK? We're going to stop right there.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, who is this lovely lady right here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Here is Rosalie Steinweis, is my wife. And I-- I met her in Poland, after the war. We came together to this country. We raised two  children. We have grandchildren. And I think we made in this a success. Because our sons-- they are educated. And that's-- this what we wanted. And we have, I would say, a good life here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sounds good to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: That.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, can you  tell us about the tattoo on your arm there.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, yeah. This number-- I got it in Blechhammer. When I came to Blechhammer, the SS took over there. And they put-- we were there 5,000 people-- numbers like that on the hand, on the left hand.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and it's number 179512.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year did you think you got it, about? It must be--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, you can figure out in Blechhammer when I was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Somewhere--

speaker: interviewee
text: --right from the beginning.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, so January of 1943, probably.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, later-- later, 1944.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, from the right to the left--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, David, who do we have in these pictures?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It's me, David Steinweis.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the right.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the-- in the middle is Yuda Steinweis-- and the left is Rachmil Steinweis.

speaker: interviewer
text: Those are your three surviving brothers from the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what pic-- when when was this picture taken about, this composite picture? It's about--

speaker: interviewer
text: And where is the picture? Where are the pictures from? All-- everybody in New York at the time of this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, New York, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: New York City, OK. Who's in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: In this picture is my sister, Dora Steinweis.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was the picture taken?

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was the picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Sosnowitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. OK, what are we looking at here? What's this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, this is the picture-- I was in a organ-- Zionist organization. And they called it a [? kibbeh. ?] And I went this time to Hashara, to a kibbutz. This locat-- it was located in Czarnow, Poland. And in this--

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: In this-- this was in the year 1933.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you are this one here in the white shirt?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'm here in the white shirt, yeah. A few people from there--

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to stop.

speaker: interviewee
text: --maybe have--

speaker: interviewer
text: What are we looking at here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this is a picture taken 1947 in Deggendorf, in West Germany. And-- and this is my older son, who is today 48. And this time, he was not even a year old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's you and your wife.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Rosalie, my wife, and me there. David Steinweis and Rosalie Steinweis.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good. And what are we looking at here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, this is the-- this is the picture from the Alte Kaserne in West Germany. Over there was a DP camp. The people from there-- they coming out to say goodbye to-- to me and to my wife, Rosalie Steinweis.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and this was-- so 1947

speaker: interviewee
text: Seven.

speaker: interviewer
text: --at Deggendorf DP camp, outside-- just outside of it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what are we looking at here?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is a goodbye picture to the people what they were together in DP camp in Deggendorf, in West Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's Rosalie in the front there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Over in the--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you're on the right hand side?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the front is my-- in the front, I am David Steinweis. And next to me--

speaker: interviewer
text: You can't see who that is?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't see that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then Rosalie after that.

speaker: interviewee
text: After them. And next to me is Rosalie Steinweis.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, great.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, where is this cemetery located?

speaker: interviewee
text: This-- this cemetery is located in Poland, in a little town near Sosnowitz called [NON-ENGLISH]. It's-- the cemetery is 250 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is where you think your mother might be buried?

speaker: interviewee
text: And yeah, my mother is there buried, on this cemetery.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the picture was taken when?

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture was taken-- two years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Last year? OK, so it would be--

speaker: interviewee
text: Last year.

speaker: interviewer
text: 1995, [INAUDIBLE]--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, 1995.

speaker: interviewer
text: --by your son, Alan.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah, this picture was taken 1995 by my youngest son, Alan Steinweis.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is on a wedding what was in 1956, in New York. From the right to the left, the first is David Steinweis. The second is Rosalie Steinweis. The-- in the middle is my older brother, Rachmil Steinweis. And next is his wife, Anna Steinweis. And next is Rosa [? Rotmensch ?] and my brother, also Yuda Steinweis, only they changed to the name [? Rotmensch. ?] Only he is my brother, and his name was before Steinweis.

speaker: interviewer
text: And on the bottom?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who are the kids?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, the are kids. Going back to the right is my oldest son Saul, my nephew Jackie, my other nephew Sam, and my niece Sheila. Sheila--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And it looks like at the time your wife is pregnant with Alan, if I had to guess.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's how I remember the year when it was taken.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's [INAUDIBLE]. OK, what are we looking at here?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the-- here, from the right side, is David Steinweis. Next is Rosalie Steinweis. Next is my oldest son and his future wife, [? Oiva. ?] And the last one is--

speaker: interviewer
text: It's your son Alan.

speaker: interviewee
text: Alan Steinweis.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and this is at your oldest son's wedding.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was December 16, 1973 in New York City.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yeah. Oh--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my--

speaker: interviewer
text: So who's in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my son, Alan Steinweis, with his wife [? Katherine. ?] And this picture was taken--

speaker: interviewer
text: In May--

speaker: interviewee
text: May 19--

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Stony Brook, Long Island.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Stony Brook, Long  Island. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Stony--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and who do we have in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, I will-- OK, I willd I will start to the left. My oldest son, Saul Steinweis. My daughter-in-law, [? Oiva ?] Steinweis. My grandchild, Gail Steinweis. The other girl-- Melanie Steinweis.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this picture was taken in--

speaker: interviewee
text: And this--

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in Stony Brook, Long Island.

speaker: interviewer
text: Stony Brook, 1993.

speaker: interviewee
text: In May, 1993.

speaker: interviewer
text: In May. Lovely family. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, thank you, yeah.
